subject: ap-chemistry
title: Gravimetric Analysis: Iodide Determination
stimulus:
A student is given the task of determining the I− content of tablets that contain KI and an inert, water-soluble
sugar as a filler. A tablet is dissolved in 50.0 mL of distilled water, and an excess of 0.20 M $Pb(NO_3)_2$(aq) is
added to the solution. A yellow precipitate forms, which is then filtered, washed, and dried. The data from the
experiment are shown in the table above.

parts.prompt:
For the chemical reaction that occurs when the precipitate forms,
(i) write a balanced, net-ionic equation for the reaction, and
(ii) explain why the reaction is best represented by a net-ionic equation.
parts.scoring_notes: Part (i): $Pb^{2+}$(aq) + 2 I$^-$(aq) → PbI$_2$(s). Must be balanced with correct states. Part (ii): The net-ionic equation shows only the species that participate in forming the precipitate; the spectator ions (K$^+$ and NO$_3^-$) cancel and are omitted.

scoring_points.description: For the correct balanced net-ionic equation: 1 point
scoring_points.official_rubric: Pb2+ + 2 I− → PbI2. 1 point is earned for a balanced net-ionic equation.

sample_responses.commentary: Correctly balanced with proper 1:2 stoichiometry, spectator ions omitted, and correct (s) state for the precipitate.
Spectator ions $K^+$ and $NO_3^-$ must be cancelled; a net-ionic equation shows only species involved in the reaction.

alternatives.correct_example: $Pb^{2+}$(aq) + 2 I$^-$(aq) → PbI$_2$(s). The yellow precipitate is lead(II) iodide, PbI$_2$.

wrong_examples: Including spectator ions K$^+$ or NO$_3^-$ — net-ionic equations cancel spectator ions.
Writing PbI$_2$(aq) — PbI$_2$ is the insoluble precipitate, so it must have (s) state.
common_traps: The coefficient ratio is 1 Pb$^{2+}$ : 2 I$^-$ because lead(II) iodide is PbI$_2$.

scoring_points.description: For a correct explanation of why a net-ionic equation is best: 1 point
scoring_points.official_rubric: The net-ionic equation shows the formation of the PbI2(s) from Pb2+(aq) and I−(aq) ions, omitting the non-reacting species (spectator ions), K+(aq) and NO3−(aq). 1 point is earned for a valid explanation.

sample_responses.response: The net-ionic equation is best because it shows only the species that actually participate in the reaction — $Pb^{2+}$ and $I^-$ — and omits the spectator ions $K^+$ and $NO_3^-$ that remain unchanged in solution.
sample_responses.commentary: Correctly identifies $K^+$ and $NO_3^-$ as spectators and explains that the net-ionic equation excludes non-reacting species.

sample_responses.response: The net-ionic equation is best because it is shorter and easier to write.
sample_responses.commentary: Brevity alone is not an acceptable justification; the explanation must reference spectator ions and their non-participation in the reaction.

required_elements: K$^+$ and NO$_3^-$ (or equivalent spectator ions) do not participate in the reaction.
Net-ionic equation shows only the reacting species.
alternatives.correct_example: The net-ionic equation best represents the reaction because the K$^+$ and NO$_3^-$ ions are spectators — they remain in solution unchanged and do not participate in forming the PbI$_2$ precipitate. Only Pb$^{2+}$ and I$^-$ are involved in the precipitation.

wrong_examples: Saying it is shorter — the explanation must reference spectator ions, not just brevity.
common_traps: Must identify specific spectator ions (K$^+$ and NO$_3^-$) or describe them as ions that don't change.

parts.prompt: Explain the purpose of drying and weighing the filter paper with the precipitate three times.
parts.scoring_notes: To ensure the precipitate has reached constant mass (all water driven off). Repeated weighings confirm no further mass change, which means drying is complete.

scoring_points.description: For correct purpose: drying to constant mass ensures all water is removed: 1 point
scoring_points.official_rubric: The filter paper and precipitate must be dried several times (to a constant mass) to ensure that all the water has been driven off. 1 point is earned for a valid explanation.

sample_responses.response: Drying and weighing multiple times ensures the precipitate reaches constant mass, confirming all water has been driven off before recording the final mass.
sample_responses.commentary: Correctly invokes the concept of constant mass and explains why repeated drying is needed to eliminate residual water from the mass measurement.

sample_responses.response: To get more precise measurements by averaging the three values.
sample_responses.commentary: The purpose is not to average; it is to verify that the precipitate has stopped losing mass (i.e., all water is gone). Averaging is not the goal.

required_elements: To ensure the precipitate has reached constant mass.
Confirms all water has been driven off so the mass measured is only PbI$_2$.
alternatives.correct_example: The filter paper and precipitate are dried and weighed multiple times to confirm constant mass. When consecutive weighings agree, it indicates that all water has evaporated. If the mass were measured only once, residual water would make the mass appear larger, leading to an overestimate of the amount of PbI$_2$.

wrong_examples: To reduce random error — this is not the primary purpose; it is specifically to verify complete drying.
common_traps: The key concept is 'constant mass' — the purpose is to verify complete drying, not just to average readings.

parts.prompt: In the filtrate solution, is [K$^+$] greater than, less than, or equal to [NO$_3^-$]? Justify your answer.
parts.scoring_notes: [K$^+$] < [NO$_3^-$]. The Pb(NO$_3$)$_2$ was added in excess, contributing NO$_3^-$ beyond what came from the KI tablet. K$^+$ came only from KI; Pb$^{2+}$ was added in excess, contributing additional NO$_3^-$ to solution.

scoring_points.description: For [K$^+$] less than [NO$_3^-$] with correct reasoning about excess Pb(NO$_3$)$_2$: 1 point
scoring_points.official_rubric: [K+] is less than [NO3−] because the source of the NO3−, the 0.20 M Pb(NO3)2(aq), was added in excess. 1 point is earned for a correct comparison with a valid explanation.

sample_responses.response: $[K^+]$ is less than $[NO_3^-]$. The $K^+$ came only from the KI tablet, while $NO_3^-$ came from the excess $Pb(NO_3)_2$ added to the solution. Because $Pb(NO_3)_2$ was in excess, additional $NO_3^-$ beyond that needed to precipitate all $I^-$ remains in solution.
sample_responses.commentary: Correctly identifies the direction of inequality and traces the extra $NO_3^-$ to the excess $Pb(NO_3)_2$ reagent.

sample_responses.response: $[K^+]$ equals $[NO_3^-]$ because the KI tablet and the $Pb(NO_3)_2$ each contribute equal ions.
sample_responses.commentary: Incorrect. The excess $Pb(NO_3)_2$ provides additional $NO_3^-$ beyond what is needed stoichiometrically, making $[NO_3^-]$ greater than $[K^+]$.

required_elements: [K$^+$] is less than [NO$_3^-$].
Pb(NO$_3$)$_2$ was added in excess, so extra NO$_3^-$ from the Pb(NO$_3$)$_2$ remains in solution.
alternatives.correct_example: [K$^+$] < [NO$_3^-$]. The K$^+$ ions came only from the dissolved KI tablet. The NO$_3^-$ ions came from the Pb(NO$_3$)$_2$ solution, which was added in excess. The excess Pb(NO$_3$)$_2$ contributes additional NO$_3^-$ (all of which remains in solution), making [NO$_3^-$] greater than [K$^+$].

wrong_examples: Saying equal — the excess Pb(NO$_3$)$_2$ adds NO$_3^-$ beyond what came from the tablet.
Saying greater — K$^+$ has no extra source; NO$_3^-$ has the excess Pb(NO$_3$)$_2$ as an extra source.
common_traps: The excess reagent is Pb(NO$_3$)$_2$, which contains 2 NO$_3^-$ per formula unit, significantly increasing [NO$_3^-$] relative to [K$^+$].

parts.prompt: Calculate the number of moles of precipitate that is produced in the experiment.
parts.scoring_notes: Mass of PbI$_2$ = (mass of filter paper + precipitate) − (mass of filter paper). Using $n = m / M$ with molar mass of PbI$_2$ = 461.0 g/mol. From the data table: mass of PbI$_2$ ≈ 0.236 g; $n$ ≈ 5.12 × 10$^{-4}$ mol.

scoring_points.description: For the correct moles of PbI$_2$ with proper setup: 1 point
scoring_points.official_rubric: 1.698 g − 1.462 g = 0.236 g PbI2(s); 0.236 g PbI2 × (1 mol PbI2 / 461.0 g PbI2) = 5.12 × 10−4 mol PbI2. 1 point is earned for the correct number of moles of PbI2(s) precipitate.

sample_responses.response:
$m(PbI_2) = 1.698\text{ g} - 1.462\text{ g} = 0.236\text{ g}$
$n = \frac{0.236\text{ g}}{461.0\text{ g/mol}} = 5.12 \times 10^{-4}\text{ mol } PbI_2$
sample_responses.commentary: Correctly subtracts empty filter paper mass to get net precipitate mass, then divides by the correct molar mass of $PbI_2$ (207.2 + 2×126.9 = 461.0 g/mol).
Used the gross mass (filter paper + precipitate) rather than subtracting the empty filter paper mass. The net precipitate mass must be calculated first.

required_elements: $n(\text{PbI}_2) = \frac{0.236\text{ g}}{461.0\text{ g/mol}} = 5.12 \times 10^{-4}\text{ mol}$
alternatives.correct_example:
$m(\text{PbI}_2) = 1.698\text{ g} - 1.462\text{ g} = 0.236\text{ g}$
$n = \frac{0.236\text{ g}}{461.0\text{ g/mol}} = 5.12 \times 10^{-4}\text{ mol PbI}_2$

wrong_examples: Using the gross mass of the filter paper + precipitate instead of subtracting the empty filter paper mass.
Using the wrong molar mass — PbI$_2$ = 207.2 + 2(126.9) = 461.0 g/mol.
common_traps: Must subtract the mass of the empty filter paper to get the net mass of precipitate only.

parts.prompt: Calculate the mass percent of I$^-$ in the tablet.

scoring_points.description: For the correct mass percent of I$^-$ consistent with part (d): 1 point
scoring_points.official_rubric: 5.12 × 10−4 mol PbI2 × (2 mol I− / 1 mol PbI2) = 1.02 × 10−3 mol I−; 1.02 × 10−3 mol I− × 126.91 g/mol = 0.130 g I−; 0.130 g / 0.425 g = 0.306 = 30.6% I− per KI tablet. 1 point earned for moles of I−; 1 point earned for mass percent.

sample_responses.response:
$n(I^-) = 2 \times 5.12\times10^{-4}\text{ mol} = 1.024\times10^{-3}\text{ mol}$
$m(I^-) = 1.024\times10^{-3}\text{ mol} \times 126.9\text{ g/mol} = 0.130\text{ g}$
$\text{Mass %} = \frac{0.130\text{ g}}{0.425\text{ g}} \times 100\% = 30.6\%$
sample_responses.commentary: Correctly applies the 2:1 mole ratio of $I^-$ to $PbI_2$, converts to mass, and divides by the tablet mass from the data table.
Incorrectly used a 1:1 mole ratio instead of 2:1. Each formula unit of $PbI_2$ contains two $I^-$ ions.

required_elements: $n(\text{I}^-) = 2 \times n(\text{PbI}_2) = 2 \times 5.12\times10^{-4} = 1.024\times10^{-3}\text{ mol}$
$m(\text{I}^-) = 1.024\times10^{-3}\text{ mol} \times 126.9\text{ g/mol} = 0.130\text{ g}$
Mass percent = $(0.130\text{ g} / 0.425\text{ g}) \times 100\% = 30.6\%$

wrong_examples: Using $n$(I$^-$) = $n$(PbI$_2$) instead of 2 × $n$(PbI$_2$) — each PbI$_2$ contains 2 iodide ions.
Not multiplying by 100 to convert to percent.
common_traps: The mole ratio of I$^-$ to PbI$_2$ is 2:1. Divide by the tablet mass (from the data table), not by the precipitate mass.

parts.prompt:
In another trial, the student dissolves a tablet in 55.0 mL of water instead of 50.0 mL of water. Predict
whether the experimentally determined mass percent of I$^-$ will be greater than, less than, or equal to the
amount calculated in part (e). Justify your answer.
parts.scoring_notes: Equal. More water does not change the total amount of I$^-$ in the tablet. The Pb(NO$_3$)$_2$ was added in excess, ensuring complete precipitation of I$^-$ regardless of volume of dissolving water. The extra water is removed during filtration and drying.

scoring_points.description: For 'equal' with correct reasoning about excess Pb(NO$_3$)$_2$: 1 point
scoring_points.official_rubric: The mass percent of I− will be the same. Pb2+(aq) was added in excess, ensuring that essentially no I− remained in solution. The additional water is removed by filtration and drying, leaving the same mass of dried precipitate. 1 point is earned for correct comparison with a valid justification.

sample_responses.response: Equal. The $Pb^{2+}$ was added in excess, so all $I^-$ is completely precipitated regardless of whether 50.0 mL or 55.0 mL of water was used. The extra water is removed by filtration and drying, and the same mass of $PbI_2$ precipitate remains, giving the same mass percent.
sample_responses.commentary: Correctly identifies that excess $Pb^{2+}$ guarantees complete precipitation and that extra water is eliminated during the drying step.

sample_responses.response: Less than. More water dilutes the $I^-$ concentration, so less precipitate forms.
sample_responses.commentary: Incorrect. Dilution does not reduce the total moles of $I^-$ in the tablet. Since $Pb^{2+}$ is in excess, all $I^-$ precipitates regardless of volume.

required_elements: Equal.
Pb(NO$_3$)$_2$ is in excess; all I$^-$ is still precipitated regardless of the additional water.
Additional water is removed during filtration/drying and does not affect the mass of dried precipitate.
alternatives.correct_example: The mass percent of I$^-$ will be equal. The Pb$^{2+}$ is in excess, so all I$^-$ ions are still precipitated as PbI$_2$ regardless of the 5.0 mL extra water. The water is removed by filtration and drying, leaving the same mass of PbI$_2$ and yielding the same calculated mass percent.

wrong_examples: Saying less than — extra water does not remove any precipitate.
Saying greater than — dilution does not increase I$^-$ content.
common_traps: The excess Pb$^{2+}$ ensures complete precipitation; extra water has no effect on the final dried precipitate mass.

parts.prompt:
A student in another lab also wants to determine the I$^-$ content of a KI tablet but does not have access to
$Pb(NO_3)_2$. However, the student does have access to 0.20 M AgNO$_3$, which reacts with I$^-$(aq) to produce
AgI(s). The value of $K_{sp}$ for AgI is 8.5 × 10$^{-17}$.
(i) Will the substitution of AgNO$_3$ for $Pb(NO_3)_2$ result in the precipitation of the I$^-$ ion from solution?
Justify your answer.
(ii) The student only has access to one KI tablet and a balance that can measure to the nearest 0.01 g.
Will the student be able to determine the mass of AgI produced to three significant figures? Justify
your answer.
parts.scoring_notes: Part (g)(i): Yes — $K_{sp}$(AgI) = 8.5 × 10$^{-17}$ is extremely small, indicating AgI is virtually insoluble; Q > $K_{sp}$ when Ag$^+$ and I$^-$ are mixed, so precipitation will occur. Part (g)(ii): $n$(AgI) ≈ 1.024 × 10$^{-3}$ mol; mass AgI = 1.024 × 10$^{-3}$ × 234.8 = 0.240 g. A balance readable to 0.01 g has only 2 sig figs for a mass of ~0.24 g — cannot determine to 3 sig figs.

scoring_points.official_rubric: Yes. Addition of an excess of 0.20 M AgNO3(aq) will precipitate all of the I− ion present in the solution because AgI is insoluble, as evidenced by its low value of Ksp. 1 point is earned for the correct answer with a valid justification.

sample_responses.response: Yes. $K_{sp}(AgI) = 8.5 \times 10^{-17}$ is very small, meaning AgI is essentially insoluble. When excess 0.20 M $Ag^+$ is added, the ion product $Q = [Ag^+][I^-]$ greatly exceeds $K_{sp}$, so virtually all $I^-$ precipitates as $AgI(s)$.
sample_responses.commentary: Correctly cites the very small $K_{sp}$ as evidence of insolubility and invokes the Q vs. $K_{sp}$ comparison to justify precipitation.

sample_responses.response: No. $AgNO_3$ is a different reagent and may not react the same way with $I^-$.
sample_responses.commentary: Incorrect. The extremely low $K_{sp}$ of AgI (8.5 × 10⁻¹⁷) demonstrates that AgI is highly insoluble; $Ag^+$ will effectively precipitate $I^-$ from solution.

required_elements: Yes, I$^-$ will precipitate.
$K_{sp}$(AgI) = 8.5 × 10$^{-17}$ is very small, meaning AgI is extremely insoluble.
alternatives.correct_example: Yes. The $K_{sp}$ for AgI (8.5 × 10$^{-17}$) is very small, indicating AgI is highly insoluble. When 0.20 M Ag$^+$ is mixed with I$^-$ from the tablet, the reaction quotient Q = [Ag$^+$][I$^-$] will exceed $K_{sp}$, driving precipitation of AgI.

wrong_examples: Saying no — the extremely small $K_{sp}$ guarantees precipitation when concentrations exceed it.
common_traps: Small $K_{sp}$ means the ion product exceeds $K_{sp}$ easily at measurable concentrations — precipitation occurs.

scoring_points.description: For correct mass calculation and conclusion about sig figs: 2 points
scoring_points.official_rubric: No. If masses can be measured to ±0.01 g, then the mass of the dry AgI(s) precipitate (which is less than 1 g) will be known to only two significant figures. 1 point is earned for a correct answer with a valid justification.

sample_responses.response:
No. $n(AgI) = n(I^-) = 1.024\times10^{-3}\text{ mol}$
$m(AgI) = 1.024\times10^{-3}\text{ mol} \times 234.8\text{ g/mol} = 0.240\text{ g}$
A balance readable to ±0.01 g has a precision of 0.01 g. For a mass of ~0.24 g, this gives only 2 significant figures (the tenths and hundredths places). The student cannot determine the mass to 3 significant figures.
sample_responses.commentary: Correctly calculates the expected mass (~0.24 g), identifies that a ±0.01 g balance cannot resolve 3 sig figs for a sub-1 g mass, and reaches the correct conclusion.

sample_responses.response: Yes. The student can weigh the filter paper and precipitate to 0.01 g precision, which is sufficient.
sample_responses.commentary: Incorrect. A mass of ~0.24 g measured to the nearest 0.01 g has only 2 significant figures. Three sig figs would require knowing the mass to the nearest 0.001 g (a balance accurate to ±0.001 g).

required_elements: Mass of AgI ≈ 0.240 g (consistent with 1.024 × 10$^{-3}$ mol × 234.8 g/mol).
A balance reading to 0.01 g gives at most 2 significant figures for ~0.24 g.
Cannot determine 3 sig figs with this balance.
alternatives.correct_example:
$n$(AgI) = $n$(I$^-$) = 2 × 5.12 × 10$^{-4}$ = 1.024 × 10$^{-3}$ mol
$m$(AgI) = 1.024 × 10$^{-3}$ mol × 234.8 g/mol = 0.240 g
A balance readable to ±0.01 g has precision of 0.01 g. For a mass of 0.24 g, this gives only 2 significant figures. The student cannot determine the mass to 3 significant figures.

wrong_examples: Saying yes, 3 sig figs are achievable — a mass of ~0.24 g read on a 0.01 g balance gives at best 2 sig figs (24 ± 1 in the last digit).
Not calculating the expected mass to justify the conclusion.
common_traps: Significant figures depend on the precision of the balance relative to the size of the measurement. For 0.24 g on a ±0.01 g balance, there are only 2 sig figs.
